{"id":"undercity-zhs","title":"Replace Owl with Ratatouille for terminal rendering","description":"## Goal

Replace the current Owl.LiveScreen-based rendering stack with Ratatouille, a declarative terminal UI framework using the Elm Architecture (TEA). This unblocks proper two-column layout (lz3) and gives us a responsive, composable view system.

## Why Replace Owl

Owl.LiveScreen blocks are strictly vertical and stacked — no side-by-side layout is possible. Achieving two-column layout in Owl requires manually composing iodata (zipping lines from left and right panels) into a single block, which is fragile and not responsive to terminal width.

Ratatouille provides a 12-column grid DSL (row/column, like HTML) where multi-column layout is first-class. Column widths are computed from actual terminal width at render time, so layouts are responsive. Two-column layout becomes:

  row do
    column(size: 9) do ... end
    column(size: 3) do ... end
  end

Two-column layout itself is OUT OF SCOPE for this bead — that is tracked in lz3, which is blocked by this one.

## Ratatouille Architecture

Implements the Elm Architecture (TEA) via three callbacks on a module that declares @behaviour Ratatouille.App:

- init/1 — receives runtime context (including window.height, window.width) and returns initial model
- update/2 — receives (model, msg) and returns new model. Messages come from: terminal events (keypresses, resize, mouse), subscription ticks, completed async commands
- render/1 — receives current model, returns a view tree rooted at a view element. Runtime calls this on each repaint.

The runtime is an OTP process that owns the render cycle (default 500ms interval). It uses ex_termbox (C NIF wrapping the termbox library) to take exclusive control of the terminal in raw mode via an alternate screen buffer.

## DSL Elements

view, row, column, panel, label, text, table, table_row, table_cell, bar, overlay, canvas, chart, tree, tree_node, sparkline

Layout example from rendering.exs:
  view(top_bar: top_bar, bottom_bar: bottom_bar) do
    panel title: 'Main', height: :fill do
      row do
        column(size: 4) do
          panel title: 'Left' do
            label(content: 'hello')
          end
        end
        column(size: 8) do
          panel title: 'Right' do
            label(content: 'world')
          end
        end
      end
    end
  end

## Async Patterns

Commands — Command.new(&fn/0, tag) runs a function async; result arrives as {tag, result} in update/2. Maps to our Gateway RPC calls (currently blocking sync_messages).

Subscriptions — Subscription.interval(ms, :message_name) delivers ticks to update/2. Maps to our sync_messages poll.

## Input Model

IMPORTANT CHANGE: Ratatouille is fully event-driven. There is no IO.gets. Every keypress arrives as {:event, %{ch: ch, key: key}} in update/2. Text input must be accumulated character by character in the model (editor.exs pattern). Enter triggers dispatch.

## Transferability

### Clean mappings
- GameState struct → TEA model (near 1:1, no change needed)
- Commands routing + modules → mostly reusable; return updated model instead of pushing to MessageBuffer
- MessageBuffer → becomes a messages: [] list field in the model; no Agent needed at all (simpler)
- View.BlockDescription, View.Status, View.Constitution → pure formatting logic is reusable; output format changes from Owl-tagged iodata to Ratatouille label/text DSL elements
- View.Surroundings 3x3 grid → rework from Owl.Table to Ratatouille table/panel DSL; hardcoded @cell_width 30 (~94 cols) goes away, widths become responsive

### Significant rework
- Input module (IO.gets) → DELETED entirely; replaced by keypress accumulation in model state (see editor.exs example)
- GameLoop → DELETED; replaced by TEA update/2 callback responding to events
- InventorySelector / TargetSelector → major rework; current blocking-receive pattern is incompatible; must become explicit model mode (e.g. model.mode = {:selecting_inventory, options}) with render/1 branching on mode and update/2 handling keypresses differently per mode
- Commands with multi-step IO (e.g. Scribble) → any IO.gets inside command modules must become model state transitions

### Terminal styling
Owl uses tagged iodata (Owl.Data.tag/2) and IO.ANSI for colours. Ratatouille uses keyword attributes on DSL elements: label(content: '...', color: :red, attributes: [:bold]). All colour/style code needs updating.

## Dependency

Ratatouille: ~> 0.5.0
Pulls in: ex_termbox (C NIF), elixir_make, asciichart

## Platform Note — CONFIRM BEFORE STARTING

ex_termbox uses a C NIF requiring POSIX terminal APIs (termios, tcsetattr for raw mode). This will NOT compile in MSYS2/Git Bash on Windows. Must be compiled and run from a POSIX environment (WSL2 recommended).

BEFORE BEGINNING IMPLEMENTATION: verify that mix compile succeeds with {:ratatouille, '~> 0.5.0'} added to deps, and that the rendering.exs example from the Ratatouille repo renders correctly in the target terminal.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T02:35:32Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-03T02:35:32Z","work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"undercity-zhs","title":"Replace Owl with Ratatouille for terminal rendering","description":"## Goal

Replace the current Owl.LiveScreen-based rendering stack with Ratatouille, a declarative terminal UI framework using the Elm Architecture (TEA). This unblocks proper two-column layout (lz3) and gives us a responsive, composable view system.

## Why Replace Owl

Owl.LiveScreen blocks are strictly vertical and stacked — no side-by-side layout is possible. Achieving two-column layout in Owl requires manually composing iodata (zipping lines from left and right panels) into a single block, which is fragile and not responsive to terminal width.

Ratatouille provides a 12-column grid DSL (row/column, like HTML) where multi-column layout is first-class. Column widths are computed from actual terminal width at render time, so layouts are responsive. Two-column layout becomes:

  row do
    column(size: 9) do ... end
    column(size: 3) do ... end
  end

Two-column layout itself is OUT OF SCOPE for this bead — that is tracked in lz3, which is blocked by this one.

## Ratatouille Architecture

Implements the Elm Architecture (TEA) via three callbacks on a module that declares @behaviour Ratatouille.App:

- init/1 — receives runtime context (including window.height, window.width) and returns initial model
- update/2 — receives (model, msg) and returns new model. Messages come from: terminal events (keypresses, resize, mouse), subscription ticks, completed async commands
- render/1 — receives current model, returns a view tree rooted at a view element. Runtime calls this on each repaint.

The runtime is an OTP process that owns the render cycle (default 500ms interval). It uses ex_termbox (C NIF wrapping the termbox library) to take exclusive control of the terminal in raw mode via an alternate screen buffer.

## DSL Elements

view, row, column, panel, label, text, table, table_row, table_cell, bar, overlay, canvas, chart, tree, tree_node, sparkline

Layout example from rendering.exs:
  view(top_bar: top_bar, bottom_bar: bottom_bar) do
    panel title: 'Main', height: :fill do
      row do
        column(size: 4) do
          panel title: 'Left' do
            label(content: 'hello')
          end
        end
        column(size: 8) do
          panel title: 'Right' do
            label(content: 'world')
          end
        end
      end
    end
  end

## Async Patterns

Commands — Command.new(&fn/0, tag) runs a function async; result arrives as {tag, result} in update/2. Maps to our Gateway RPC calls (currently blocking sync_messages).

Subscriptions — Subscription.interval(ms, :message_name) delivers ticks to update/2. Maps to our sync_messages poll.

## Input Model

{"id":"undercity-6uh","title":"Selector model-state transitions for TEA compatibility","description":"## Context

Part of the Owl → Ratatouille migration (undercity-zhs). The core runtime is complete and Owl is fully removed. The remaining broken piece is that `drop`, `eat`, and `attack` call `InventorySelector.select` and `TargetSelector.select`, which use `IO.gets` — this blocks the Ratatouille event loop and is incompatible with TEA.

## Goal

Replace the blocking IO.gets selection pattern with TEA model state transitions. From the user's perspective the UX is unchanged: type a command, an overlay appears with a numbered list, type a number + Enter to confirm.

## Branch

Do this work on the existing `zhs-replace-owl` branch.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. New return type from Commands

Add `{:needs_selection, :inventory | :target, items, continuation}` (or similar) as a return type from `Commands.dispatch`. Commands that currently call a selector synchronously should instead return this tuple with the items to show and enough context for App to complete the action once selection is done.

Affected command modules: `Commands.Drop`, `Commands.Eat`, `Commands.Attack`.

`Attack` calls InventorySelector (weapon) then TargetSelector (target) — two sequential selections. Use two modes: `{:selecting_weapon, items}` → `{:selecting_target, weapon, people}`.

### 2. Model mode field

Add `mode` to the model in `UndercityCli.App.init/1`:

```elixir
mode: :normal
# also possible values:
# {:selecting_inventory, items, action_fn}
# {:selecting_weapon, items}
# {:selecting_target, weapon, people}
```

Where `action_fn` is a function `index -> {:moved | :continue, new_state}` that completes the gateway call once an item is chosen.

### 3. dispatch_input changes

When `Commands.dispatch` returns `{:needs_selection, ...}`, set `model.mode` accordingly instead of calling the gateway. Clear input.

When mode is a selector mode and the user presses Enter:
- Run `InventorySelector.parse_choice/2` (pure logic, no IO — keep this function)
- On valid choice: call the gateway, apply state, return to `:normal` mode
- On invalid choice: stay in selector mode (model unchanged except input cleared)
- On cancel choice (last option): return to `:normal` mode

### 4. Render overlay

In `UndercityCli.App.render/1`, when `model.mode != :normal`, wrap the view in an `overlay` with a panel showing the numbered options list.

```elixir
# sketch
if model.mode != :normal do
  overlay do
    panel title: "Choose", padding: @panel_padding do
      # numbered label per option
    end
  end
end
```

Ratatouille's `overlay/1` floats over the existing view — no layout changes needed.

### 5. InventorySelector / TargetSelector

After this work, `read_loop/1` in both modules becomes dead code and should be deleted. `parse_choice/2` is kept (pure logic, has tests). The `select/2` entry points are no longer called from commands — remove them or leave as dead stubs to be cleaned in the arrow-key rework (undercity-yjo).

- `apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands/eat.ex`

- `apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view/inventory_selector.ex`
- `apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view/target_selector.ex`

{"design":"## Revised Design

### Model

Single consolidated model replaces both `GameState` and the App model struct. `GameState` is deleted.

```elixir
%{
  player_id, player_name,   # identity
  vicinity, ap, hp,         # game state
  input, messages,          # input/output
  gateway, window_width,    # runtime deps
  pending: nil              # selection state (see below)
}
```

`pending` is either `nil` (normal mode) or:
```elixir
%{command: "attack", args: [person], label: "Attack with what?", choices: [items...]}
```

### Selectors

Commands set their own pending context before handing off to the selector:

```elixir
model
|> Model.pending("attack", [person])   # sets command + collected args
|> Model.select("Attack with what?", items)  # adds label + choices
```

App re-dispatches uniformly on selection — no callbacks, no closures:
```elixir
Commands.dispatch(pending.command, pending.args ++ [chosen], model)
```

Overlay is rendered when `model.pending != nil`. Render reads `pending.label` and `pending.choices`.

### Dispatch signatures

Commands take and return the model directly. No `{:continue, state}` / `{:moved, state}` tags. `gateway` accessed via `model.gateway`.

```
# Move (single step)
dispatch(verb, model) → update model.vicinity, model.ap

# Inventory / Search / Help / Scribble (single step)
dispatch(verb, model) → update model (messages etc.)

# Drop
dispatch("drop", model)         → Model.pending + Model.select
dispatch("drop", index, model)  → update model.ap

# Eat
dispatch("eat", model)          → Model.pending + Model.select
dispatch("eat", index, model)   → update model.ap, model.hp

# Attack
dispatch("attack", model)                    → Model.pending + Model.select (target)
dispatch("attack", person, model)            → fetch inventory → Model.pending + Model.select (weapon)
dispatch("attack", person, index, model)     → update model.ap
```

### What changes

- `GameState` deleted — model is the single carrier
- `{:continue, state}` / `{:moved, state}` return tags removed
- `Commands.dispatch` signatures take `model` as last arg instead of `(state, gateway, message_buffer)`
- `App.dispatch_input` simplified — one pipeline, no case statement on return type
- `InventorySelector.select/2` and `TargetSelector.select/2` (blocking IO) deleted — `parse_choice/2` kept
- `read_loop/1` deleted from both selector modules"}

{"id":"undercity-lz3","title":"Two-column CLI layout with persistent message log","description":"Rework the terminal layout into two columns:

Left column (~80%): holds everything currently rendered — surroundings grid, block description, selectors, and input prompt.

Right column (~20%): persistent scrolling message log. Messages accumulate rather than clearing each loop iteration. Once the column is full (max based roughly on the height of the left column), new messages push the oldest off the top. Behaves like a ring buffer — most recent messages always visible, history scrolls out.

This replaces the current flush-and-clear model in MessageBuffer / View.render_messages. MessageBuffer's push API stays the same but the render side changes to append rather than replace.

Key open questions before implementation:
- How does Owl.LiveScreen support multi-column layout? Check if a side-by-side panel arrangement is supported or if a custom approach is needed.
- What is the terminal width assumption (fixed 80 cols, or dynamic)?
- Max message count: derive from terminal height (e.g. rows - header rows) or a fixed constant?

Relevant files:
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view.ex — View.init, render_messages
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view/screen.ex — Owl.LiveScreen wrapper
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/message_buffer.ex — current flush model
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/game_loop.ex — sync_messages, loop","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-01T15:07:00Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-01T15:07:00Z","work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"undercity-yjo","title":"Rework selectors to arrow-key navigation","description":"Currently the inventory and target selectors use numbered input (type a number + Enter). Rework them to use arrow key (up/down) navigation with Enter to confirm and Esc to cancel — a more polished terminal UX.

## Scope

Replace the numbered input model in the selector modes of UndercityCli.App with highlighted-item navigation:
- Up/Down arrows move a cursor through the options list
- Enter confirms the highlighted item
- Esc cancels and returns to :normal mode
- The overlay render highlights the current cursor position

## Relevant components

### Ratatouille
- `Ratatouille.Constants` — key constants for arrow keys (:arrow_up, :arrow_down) and Esc (:key_escape); import via `import Ratatouille.Constants, only: [key: 1]`
- `overlay` DSL element — wraps the selector panel, already in use from the numbered-input implementation

### Undercity
- `UndercityCli.App` — `update/2` handles `{:event, %{key: key}}` events; selector mode branches go here. Model gains a `cursor` integer field within each selector mode tuple.
- `UndercityCli.App.render/1` — selector overlay rendering; highlight the cursor row using `color: :yellow` or `attributes: [:bold]` on the focused label.
- `UndercityCli.View.InventorySelector` and `UndercityCli.View.TargetSelector` — `parse_choice/2` and `read_loop/1` are removed entirely; only the empty-list guard and build_options logic (if still useful) carry over.

## Depends on
Completion of the selector model-state-transition work (item 3 of undercity-zhs).","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:50Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-04T02:15:50Z"}

{"id":"undercity-dmux","title":"Unify dispatch flow: remove redispatch and make Commands.dispatch the single entry point","description":"Currently App has two separate dispatch paths: dispatch_input/1 for fresh text input (parses raw string, calls Commands.dispatch/2) and confirm_selection/3 for after a selector is confirmed (calls Commands.redispatch/3 via apply/3 with accumulated args). This split is unnecessary.

## Agreed design

Commands.dispatch/1 (taking only state) becomes the single entry point for all dispatch — both fresh input and selector confirmation. App always calls the same function regardless of whether a selection just happened.

Internally, Commands.dispatch/1 checks state.pending:
- If nil: parse state.input as usual and route to the appropriate command module
- If set: use pending.command to route to the right module. The command module then inspects pending.args to determine which pass it is on and what it still needs.

Command modules own their full selection state machine via pattern matching on pending.args:
- No args accumulated yet → first pass, show selector
- Some args accumulated → intermediate pass (e.g. attack has target but needs weapon)
- All args present → execute

## What goes away
- Commands.redispatch/3 and its apply/3 runtime arity risk
- confirm_selection/3 in App (or it becomes trivial)
- pending.command being used by App for routing (App no longer needs to know which module to re-enter)

## Result
App becomes simpler — one dispatch call, no routing logic. The post-dispatch pipeline duplication in a8v should also resolve naturally since there is only one dispatch path.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T13:23:32Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-06T13:23:32Z"}

{"notes":"Root cause: player_idle_timeout_ms is set to 50ms in test config. In the flaky test (line 51), the last GenServer call to the player process is Player.move_to inside Actions.Movement.move. After that returns, the caller still executes Block.join, Vicinity.build (multiple GenServer calls), and the full return chain. If this exceeds 50ms (CI load, GC pause), the player process fires its idle timeout, exits :normal, deregisters its name, and :sys.replace_state fails with :noproc. Fix: replace :sys.replace_state with Player.move_to(player_id, \"plaza\"). This resets the idle timer, updates both in-memory state and DETS atomically, and is race-free. Even if the process later times out and restarts, it loads the stale block_id from DETS and still exercises the scan path."}

{"notes":"## Server Expert Notes

The architecture has no transactions and cannot be given them cheaply. Every step in both sequences is a separate synchronous GenServer.call to a separate process. The fix must be compensating logic, not an atomic primitive.

Movement has three distinct failure windows, not one: (1) crash between Block.leave and Player.move_to — player absent from both blocks with stale location record; (2) crash between Player.move_to and Block.join — location record updated to destination but presence not yet recorded there (DETS and in-memory Block set inconsistent); (3) crash after Block.join but before Vicinity.build returns — relatively harmless. Windows 1 and 2 are meaningfully different and may need different recovery strategies.

Session already has partial recovery logic (scan_or_restore) that does a serial Block.has_person? scan on reconnect. If a more principled recovery path is introduced, scan_or_restore should be rationalised or removed — otherwise the project has two overlapping recovery strategies with no documented relationship.

Block.join returns block info ({block_id, [player_ids]}) that is immediately discarded in movement.ex line 24. Vicinity.build then makes a second call to Block.info to fetch the same data. If movement is restructured, this redundant call is worth collapsing.

For attack: the race window is between validate_target and Player.take_damage, with AP spent in between. The target's GenServer can stop between those two calls (idle timeout or crash) — currently Player.take_damage on an unregistered process would raise an exit with no explicit guard. The corruption is financial (AP lost on the attacker), not positional, and requires a compensating refund path in resolve_attack.

The fix must preserve the Player.location invariant documented on Player.move_to (@doc states move_to is always called before Block.join). Any reordering of steps must not violate this — location is the source of truth used by Session and tested independently.

Block GenServers do not restart with clean presence state — they reload from DETS, which includes whatever presence list was last persisted. A block crashing mid-leave or mid-join persists the corrupt list. Boot-time reconciliation against Player records does not exist. This is a known boundary — the fix here should document that it does not address block-restart recovery.

Tests will need to simulate mid-sequence process crashes (stopping a GenServer between calls). Existing Test.Helpers setup should be verified to reach individual Block GenServers by name for integration-level tests that assert on post-crash state.

## CLI Expert Notes

The CLI treats every successful gateway response as ground truth and updates state.vicinity or state.ap in place with no subsequent verification. After a move, Commands.Move does %{state | vicinity: new_vicinity, ap: new_ap} — the returned Vicinity becomes authoritative for all future commands. If the server crashes mid-move, the next command passes the stale state.vicinity.id to the gateway, hits :not_in_block, and the player has no way to recover — every action fails with no resync path.

The CLI assumes a gateway response is fully committed. There is no optimistic rollback, no retry, and no reconciliation tick. The 500ms sync_messages subscription polls for inbox messages but does not re-fetch vicinity or constitution — corrupt server state is never self-healing from the client side.

If the atomicity fix changes the shape of any gateway return value, the pattern matches in Commands.Move and Commands.Attack will need corresponding clauses. Any new error atom for 'mutation failed and was rolled back' must be added to Commands.handle_action/3 — that is the single normalisation point across all commands. If the error propagates as an unmatched tuple instead, handle_action/3 will pass it to the command-specific callback which will almost certainly raise FunctionClauseError and crash the session.

There is currently no mechanism for the CLI to request a fresh copy of the player's current location from the server. Adding a resync gateway function (returning the player's current vicinity regardless of action) would make the client resilient to any future partial failure, not just the ones fixed by this bead. This may belong in a follow-on bead but the developer should be aware of the gap."}

{"notes":"## Core Expert Notes

Current item names in active use: 'Iron Pipe', 'Chalk', 'Mushroom', 'Junk'. Each is an independent string literal with no cross-reference to any authoritative source.

- 'Iron Pipe' must appear identically in LootTable (findable item) and Combat.Weapon (@registry key). Divergence is silent — weapon registry entry becomes unreachable, or item drops but can't be used as a weapon.
- 'Mushroom' must appear identically in LootTable and Food. A mismatch causes the item to appear in inventory but return :not_edible silently.
- 'Chalk' has a default uses count of 5 embedded inline in LootTable as {'Chalk', 5}. That default is not declared anywhere canonical — anyone adjusting the uses count must know to look in LootTable, and tests hardcoding 5 will silently pass against an outdated expectation.
- 'Junk' is the default loot fallback and a generic test item. Its semantic role (no special properties) is asserted only by absence from weapon/food registries. That can only break silently.

The loot_table_test.exs tests directly pattern-match raw table entries ([{0.20, {'Chalk', 5}}, ...]). These would continue to pass after a rename that breaks runtime behaviour because they test internal table shape, not cross-module contracts.

**Proposed catalogue:** A new module UndercityCore.Item.Catalogue (or UndercityCore.Catalogue) should be the single place where each item is declared, including name, optional default uses count, and domain flags (weapon, edible). Dependency graph: Catalogue <- LootTable, Catalogue <- Food, Catalogue <- Combat.Weapon. The catalogue should live in undercity_core alongside item.ex and have no dependency on those modules.

**Canonical identifier should be an atom, not a string.** Atoms are compile-time constants; a misspelled atom fails at the call site. The string representation for display and Item.t() should be derived from the catalogue entry, not duplicated at every use site.

**Migration per module:**
- LootTable: loot entries reference atoms (:iron_pipe, :chalk etc), roll/2 resolves through catalogue to construct Item.t(). The item_spec type and build_item/1 are the specific targets.
- Combat.Weapon: @registry key becomes a catalogue atom. Public API (stats/1, weapon?/1) currently accepts String.t() from item.name — simpler path is to keep accepting strings and have Weapon do a single catalogue lookup internally. Avoids cascading changes into Inventory.find_item/2 and the server layer.
- Food: same structure as weapon registry, same migration approach.
- Item.new/1 and Item.new/2 accept arbitrary strings and need not change — the catalogue controls only well-known items.

**Migration risks:**
- LootTable tests directly pattern-match raw table entries and must be rewritten to assert on Item.t() values from roll/2. This is desirable but non-trivial.
- Item.t().name is a String.t() used for inventory lookup everywhere in the server and CLI. The catalogue's string names are effectively part of the public API once adopted — changing a display name breaks inventory lookups.
- 'Junk' decision: either all items in production loot tables must be catalogued, or the catalogue explicitly covers only items with non-trivial domain properties (weapons, edibles, consumables). The latter is defensible but must be documented.

**Tests needed:**
- UndercityCore.CatalogueTest: every item name from LootTable (across all block types) is resolvable from the catalogue; every weapon registry item is catalogued; every food table item is catalogued.
- For each catalogued item: constructing via the catalogue and via Item.new(name) produces structs with identical name fields.
- Replace raw loot_table_test.exs structure assertions with roll/2 output assertions (correct Item.t() name and uses count for a known roll value)."}

{"notes":"## CLI Expert Notes

**What breaks and why:** BlockDescription.render/2 calls Map.fetch!(@descriptions, description_key(vicinity)) on line 25 against a compile-time 7-key map. If a new block type atom is added to WorldMap and a block of that type is placed in the world, description_key/1 returns the new atom and Map.fetch! raises KeyError, crashing the render loop. Because render is called from App.render/1 inside the Ratatouille TEA loop, the crash kills the terminal client entirely rather than displaying an error message.

**Fallback vs. extension point:** Two defensible approaches:
1. Pure fallback (correct scope for this bead): Add a catch-all to description_key/1 returning :unknown, add a matching entry in @descriptions with generic copy, add a fallback clause to block_prefix/1 even though one already exists. Minimal fix — player sees generic text for an unrecognised type rather than crashing.
2. Extension point (optional follow-on): Move descriptions out of the compile-time map into function clauses (private describe/1 with per-type clauses), more idiomatic and easier to extend. The catch-all clause is still mandatory as a safety net regardless.

The bead title says 'resilient', not 'extensible' — option 1 is the correct scope. If option 1 is chosen, the description map should be documented so future block-type authors know they must add an entry.

**scribble_surface/1 should be treated the same way:** The current catch-all on line 76 silently returns 'on the ground' for any unrecognised type — this is already the right behaviour. However it should be marked with a comment as intentionally permissive, not a forgotten case. No functional change needed there.

**block_prefix/1 and description_key/1 asymmetry:** block_prefix/1 already has a catch-all; description_key/1 does not. The fix should make both consistently defensive. Tests should verify both behaviours for the same unknown-type input.

**Surroundings:** Does not use @descriptions and does not pattern-match on block type — calls Vicinity.name_for/1 and Vicinity.building?/1 which degrade gracefully for unknown block IDs. No code change needed there, but worth a read-through during review.

**Decoupling constraint:** The fix must not import or reference UndercityCore.BlockType in BlockDescription to enumerate types — that would violate the CLI decoupling rule. Use a catch-all pattern clause, not a type-driven exhaustive check.

**Missing tests to add:**
- render/2 with %Vicinity{type: :unknown_type, building_type: nil, scribble: nil} asserts it returns without raising and output contains a fallback description string.
- render/2 with same unknown type but non-nil scribble asserts the scribble line is still rendered (exercises scribble_surface/1 catch-all).
- block_prefix/1 with unknown type can be tested indirectly through the render/2 tests above since it is private."}

{"notes":"## Server Expert Notes

**The two patterns exactly:**

perform/3 path (move, search, attack): Two hops. First sends {:spend_ap, cost} into Player GenServer → Player.exert/3 (checks HP non-zero, applies lazy AP regen, calls ActionPoints.spend/3) → if AP sufficient, persists and replies {:ok, remaining_ap}. Then the action closure runs in the calling process (Actions module), wrapped in {:ok, result, ap}.

Inline handle_call path (drop, eat): Never calls Player.perform/3. Actions dispatch directly to Player.drop_item/2 or Player.eat_item/2, which send distinct messages ({:drop_item, index} or {:eat_item, index}) into the GenServer. Those callbacks call Player.drop/3 or Player.eat/3 domain functions, which internally call exert/3 themselves — AP check and state mutation happen atomically inside a single GenServer call.

Scribble is a third sub-pattern: Player.use_item/3 with a cost argument, sending {:use_item, item_name, cost} as a single atomic GenServer call combining item consumption and AP spend. The block side-effect (writing) happens after the call returns. Closer to perform/3 spirit (AP + effect separated) but the GenServer call bundles AP and item atomicity.

**Key behavioural differences:**
- Atomicity: inline path has AP check and mutation atomic in one GenServer call; perform/3 path spends AP first then runs the closure — if the closure fails after AP is spent, AP is gone but the world-state change may not have happened.
- Return shape: inline path returns action-specific flat tuples ({:ok, item_name, ap} for drop; {:ok, item, effect, ap, hp} for eat). perform/3 always returns {:ok, result, ap}.
- Both paths signal :exhausted and :collapsed the same way. The HP-zero check lives in Player.exert/3 (domain layer) for both paths — no gap there.

**What a unified pattern needs to look like:**

Three options:
1. Cosmetic only: same calling convention at the Actions layer, keep inline callbacks for actions where Player state and AP must be co-mutated. Document intentional split.
2. Closures into GenServer: perform/3 sends the closure into the GenServer itself (exert then execute closure against player state, persist, reply). True atomicity parity. More invasive.
3. Middle path: keep inline callbacks but standardise the return shape to {: ok, result, ap} across all actions, and document that the two wiring patterns are intentional for atomicity reasons.

The bead must resolve which option is in scope. Option 2 is the only path to true atomicity parity but has implications for GenServer interface and testability.

**Risks during migration:**
- If drop and eat are migrated to perform/3 externally, a genuine atomicity gap opens: AP spent in one GenServer call, inventory/health mutated in a second. Existing 'item untouched when exhausted' tests would still pass, but mid-crash behaviour cannot easily be tested with current infrastructure.
- Player.eat_item/2 currently returns {:ok, item, effect, ap, hp} — a 5-element tuple. If eat moves to perform/3, the result payload must carry {item, effect, hp} as a sub-tuple, and both Gateway and Commands.Eat must be updated to destructure accordingly.
- constitution/1 applies ActionPoints.regenerate/2 but does not persist the regenerated AP — a minor inconsistency not introduced by this migration but worth clarifying in the bead.

**Tests needed:**
- Drop returns :exhausted and leaves item in inventory (already exists — any migration must keep it green).
- Eat returns :exhausted and leaves both AP and item unchanged (already exists).
- New test for whichever unified path is introduced: proves AP and inventory/health mutation are consistent across happy path and each failure mode.
- If eat/drop return shape changes to {:ok, result, ap}, new Gateway-level tests needed — existing gateway tests do not cover eat or drop end-to-end.

## CLI Expert Notes

Every CLI command that costs AP routes through Commands.handle_action/3, which catches {:error, :exhausted} and {:error, :collapsed} and renders the appropriate message. The assumption baked into that design is that all AP-costing gateway calls can return those error atoms. In practice, three different AP-gating shapes exist behind the single handle_action/3 abstraction:

1. Gateway.perform/4 (most actions): block guard + AP via Player.perform/3
2. Gateway.drop_item/2 (defdelegate to Player.drop_item/2): no block guard, AP via internal Player call
3. :eat via perform/4: no block guard (special-cased at top of perform/4), AP via Player.eat_item/2

The CLI handles all three the same way and gets away with it because the terminal error atoms are consistent — but the path to those atoms is not, and command authors have no written rule about which gateway path to use for a new AP-costing command. 'Eat' is the confusion case: it is inventory-only yet goes through perform/4 with a special-case bypass, so a new author reading the gateway would not derive a consistent rule.

After consolidation, the CLI itself likely needs no code changes — Commands.handle_action/3 already handles :exhausted, :collapsed, and :not_in_block correctly. The contract the CLI depends on is just that those atoms are returned reliably when conditions are met.

The one edge to verify: if consolidation moves drop_item through the same Player.perform/3 wrapper as everything else, the return shape must stay {:ok, item_name, new_ap} / {:error, :invalid_index}. Commands.Drop pattern-matches on exactly those. Any change to the ok-tuple structure is a breaking change for the CLI."}

{"id":"undercity-uo1o","title":"Consolidate AP-gating into a single authoritative pattern","description":"Two incompatible AP-gating patterns exist with no documented invariant. move, search, scribble, and attack go through Player.perform/3 which applies the collapse check, lazy regen, and spend in sequence. eat and drop gate AP inline inside Player.Server GenServer callbacks, bypassing perform/3. A developer adding a new action has no guidance on which pattern to follow. Picking the wrong one silently omits either the collapse guard or the regen step. The perform/3 path is documented in docs/actions.md but the inline exceptions are not explained there.","notes":"## Server Expert Notes

**The two patterns exactly:**

perform/3 path (move, search, attack): Two hops. First sends {:spend_ap, cost} into Player GenServer → Player.exert/3 (checks HP non-zero, applies lazy AP regen, calls ActionPoints.spend/3) → if AP sufficient, persists and replies {:ok, remaining_ap}. Then the action closure runs in the calling process (Actions module), wrapped in {:ok, result, ap}.

Inline handle_call path (drop, eat): Never calls Player.perform/3. Actions dispatch directly to Player.drop_item/2 or Player.eat_item/2, which send distinct messages ({:drop_item, index} or {:eat_item, index}) into the GenServer. Those callbacks call Player.drop/3 or Player.eat/3 domain functions, which internally call exert/3 themselves — AP check and state mutation happen atomically inside a single GenServer call.

Scribble is a third sub-pattern: Player.use_item/3 with a cost argument, sending {:use_item, item_name, cost} as a single atomic GenServer call combining item consumption and AP spend. The block side-effect (writing) happens after the call returns. Closer to perform/3 spirit (AP + effect separated) but the GenServer call bundles AP and item atomicity.

**Key behavioural differences:**
- Atomicity: inline path has AP check and mutation atomic in one GenServer call; perform/3 path spends AP first then runs the closure — if the closure fails after AP is spent, AP is gone but the world-state change may not have happened.
- Return shape: inline path returns action-specific flat tuples ({:ok, item_name, ap} for drop; {:ok, item, effect, ap, hp} for eat). perform/3 always returns {:ok, result, ap}.
- Both paths signal :exhausted and :collapsed the same way. The HP-zero check lives in Player.exert/3 (domain layer) for both paths — no gap there.

**What a unified pattern needs to look like:**

Three options:
1. Cosmetic only: same calling convention at the Actions layer, keep inline callbacks for actions where Player state and AP must be co-mutated. Document intentional split.
2. Closures into GenServer: perform/3 sends the closure into the GenServer itself (exert then execute closure against player state, persist, reply). True atomicity parity. More invasive.
3. Middle path: keep inline callbacks but standardise the return shape to {: ok, result, ap} across all actions, and document that the two wiring patterns are intentional for atomicity reasons.

The bead must resolve which option is in scope. Option 2 is the only path to true atomicity parity but has implications for GenServer interface and testability.

**Risks during migration:**
- If drop and eat are migrated to perform/3 externally, a genuine atomicity gap opens: AP spent in one GenServer call, inventory/health mutated in a second. Existing 'item untouched when exhausted' tests would still pass, but mid-crash behaviour cannot easily be tested with current infrastructure.
- Player.eat_item/2 currently returns {:ok, item, effect, ap, hp} — a 5-element tuple. If eat moves to perform/3, the result payload must carry {item, effect, hp} as a sub-tuple, and both Gateway and Commands.Eat must be updated to destructure accordingly.
- constitution/1 applies ActionPoints.regenerate/2 but does not persist the regenerated AP — a minor inconsistency not introduced by this migration but worth clarifying in the bead.

**Tests needed:**
- Drop returns :exhausted and leaves item in inventory (already exists — any migration must keep it green).
- Eat returns :exhausted and leaves both AP and item unchanged (already exists).
- New test for whichever unified path is introduced: proves AP and inventory/health mutation are consistent across happy path and each failure mode.
- If eat/drop return shape changes to {:ok, result, ap}, new Gateway-level tests needed — existing gateway tests do not cover eat or drop end-to-end.

## CLI Expert Notes

Every CLI command that costs AP routes through Commands.handle_action/3, which catches {:error, :exhausted} and {:error, :collapsed} and renders the appropriate message. The assumption baked into that design is that all AP-costing gateway calls can return those error atoms. In practice, three different AP-gating shapes exist behind the single handle_action/3 abstraction:

1. Gateway.perform/4 (most actions): block guard + AP via Player.perform/3
2. Gateway.drop_item/2 (defdelegate to Player.drop_item/2): no block guard, AP via internal Player call
3. :eat via perform/4: no block guard (special-cased at top of perform/4), AP via Player.eat_item/2

The CLI handles all three the same way and gets away with it because the terminal error atoms are consistent — but the path to those atoms is not, and command authors have no written rule about which gateway path to use for a new AP-costing command. 'Eat' is the confusion case: it is inventory-only yet goes through perform/4 with a special-case bypass, so a new author reading the gateway would not derive a consistent rule.

After consolidation, the CLI itself likely needs no code changes — Commands.handle_action/3 already handles :exhausted, :collapsed, and :not_in_block correctly. The contract the CLI depends on is just that those atoms are returned reliably when conditions are met.

The one edge to verify: if consolidation moves drop_item through the same Player.perform/3 wrapper as everything else, the return shape must stay {:ok, item_name, new_ap} / {:error, :invalid_index}. Commands.Drop pattern-matches on exactly those. Any change to the ok-tuple structure is a breaking change for the CLI.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T13:44:32Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-08T13:50:40Z"}

### Doc format agreed
- Single file: docs/map.md (not city_map.md)
- ASCII art district-level map where each cell = one building/yard-sized block
- Coordinate system: letter columns (A, B, C...) + number rows (1, 2, 3...) e.g. B3, D5

- Each cell in the ASCII map represents a district zone, not an individual block

### Doc structure
- docs/map.md — top-level city map with coordinate grid, overview paragraph, district list with coords
- docs/districts/<name>.md — one file per district, each with an overview paragraph
- This two-tier approach scales from 3x3 to 100x100 without the map becoming unreadable

### Setting notes
- Civilization has collapsed — ruined city, not a living one (per STYLE_GUIDE.md)
- Refs: Berserk, Dark Souls, Between Two Fires
- Districts describe what places WERE, not what they do now
- No city name needed — narrative/lore aspects are out of scope

### City composition sketched
Likely districts for a ruined medieval city in this setting:
- Citadel/Keep (elevated, most fortified)
- Cathedral/Civic quarter
- High residential (merchant class)
- Market/Craft district
- Residential (lower, mostly collapsed)
- Gates and walls (hard boundary)
- Undercity (low-status fringe, backed against walls, lowest elevation)

### First step
Create docs/map.md and docs/districts/*.md with an initial city layout. Each block on the map = one building or building-yard-sized space. Map should be large enough to accommodate expansion up to ~100x100 individual blocks total, with districts acting as zones within that space."}

{"id":"undercity-zgqs","title":"Make BlockDescription resilient to new block types","description":"BlockDescription.render/2 calls Map.fetch\! against a closed compile-time @descriptions map with 7 keys. description_key/1 derives keys via the pattern :"space_\#{bt}", so adding any new building type to WorldMap produces a key absent from @descriptions and raises a KeyError at render time, crashing the Ratatouille update loop mid-session. scribble_surface/1 has the opposite problem: a catch-all clause that silently falls through to 'on the ground' for unknown types. The two behaviours are inconsistent. Tests only cover the 7 existing types, so neither failure mode would be caught before reaching a live session.","notes":"## CLI Expert Notes

**What breaks and why:** BlockDescription.render/2 calls Map.fetch!(@descriptions, description_key(vicinity)) on line 25 against a compile-time 7-key map. If a new block type atom is added to WorldMap and a block of that type is placed in the world, description_key/1 returns the new atom and Map.fetch! raises KeyError, crashing the render loop. Because render is called from App.render/1 inside the Ratatouille TEA loop, the crash kills the terminal client entirely rather than displaying an error message.

**Fallback vs. extension point:** Two defensible approaches:
1. Pure fallback (correct scope for this bead): Add a catch-all to description_key/1 returning :unknown, add a matching entry in @descriptions with generic copy, add a fallback clause to block_prefix/1 even though one already exists. Minimal fix — player sees generic text for an unrecognised type rather than crashing.
2. Extension point (optional follow-on): Move descriptions out of the compile-time map into function clauses (private describe/1 with per-type clauses), more idiomatic and easier to extend. The catch-all clause is still mandatory as a safety net regardless.

The bead title says 'resilient', not 'extensible' — option 1 is the correct scope. If option 1 is chosen, the description map should be documented so future block-type authors know they must add an entry.

**scribble_surface/1 should be treated the same way:** The current catch-all on line 76 silently returns 'on the ground' for any unrecognised type — this is already the right behaviour. However it should be marked with a comment as intentionally permissive, not a forgotten case. No functional change needed there.

**block_prefix/1 and description_key/1 asymmetry:** block_prefix/1 already has a catch-all; description_key/1 does not. The fix should make both consistently defensive. Tests should verify both behaviours for the same unknown-type input.

**Surroundings:** Does not use @descriptions and does not pattern-match on block type — calls Vicinity.name_for/1 and Vicinity.building?/1 which degrade gracefully for unknown block IDs. No code change needed there, but worth a read-through during review.

**Decoupling constraint:** The fix must not import or reference UndercityCore.BlockType in BlockDescription to enumerate types — that would violate the CLI decoupling rule. Use a catch-all pattern clause, not a type-driven exhaustive check.

**Missing tests to add:**
- render/2 with %Vicinity{type: :unknown_type, building_type: nil, scribble: nil} asserts it returns without raising and output contains a fallback description string.
- render/2 with same unknown type but non-nil scribble asserts the scribble line is still rendered (exercises scribble_surface/1 catch-all).
- block_prefix/1 with unknown type can be tested indirectly through the render/2 tests above since it is private.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T13:44:43Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-08T13:47:58Z"}

{"id":"undercity-2f42","title":"Introduce a canonical item catalogue to replace scattered string literals","description":"Item identity is defined implicitly by string literals scattered across multiple modules. Weapon checks 'Iron Pipe' against its own @registry. LootTable emits 'Iron Pipe', 'Mushroom', and 'Chalk' as bare strings. Food checks 'Mushroom' against its own @table. None reference each other. A rename in one place silently breaks another — the compiler produces no signal, and the failure is a quiet :not_a_weapon or :not_edible return at runtime. The loot table test even hardcodes 'Iron Pipe' as a string literal, so a registry mismatch would not be caught by tests.","notes":"## Core Expert Notes

Current item names in active use: 'Iron Pipe', 'Chalk', 'Mushroom', 'Junk'. Each is an independent string literal with no cross-reference to any authoritative source.

- 'Iron Pipe' must appear identically in LootTable (findable item) and Combat.Weapon (@registry key). Divergence is silent — weapon registry entry becomes unreachable, or item drops but can't be used as a weapon.
- 'Mushroom' must appear identically in LootTable and Food. A mismatch causes the item to appear in inventory but return :not_edible silently.
- 'Chalk' has a default uses count of 5 embedded inline in LootTable as {'Chalk', 5}. That default is not declared anywhere canonical — anyone adjusting the uses count must know to look in LootTable, and tests hardcoding 5 will silently pass against an outdated expectation.
- 'Junk' is the default loot fallback and a generic test item. Its semantic role (no special properties) is asserted only by absence from weapon/food registries. That can only break silently.

The loot_table_test.exs tests directly pattern-match raw table entries ([{0.20, {'Chalk', 5}}, ...]). These would continue to pass after a rename that breaks runtime behaviour because they test internal table shape, not cross-module contracts.

**Proposed catalogue:** A new module UndercityCore.Item.Catalogue (or UndercityCore.Catalogue) should be the single place where each item is declared, including name, optional default uses count, and domain flags (weapon, edible). Dependency graph: Catalogue <- LootTable, Catalogue <- Food, Catalogue <- Combat.Weapon. The catalogue should live in undercity_core alongside item.ex and have no dependency on those modules.

**Canonical identifier should be an atom, not a string.** Atoms are compile-time constants; a misspelled atom fails at the call site. The string representation for display and Item.t() should be derived from the catalogue entry, not duplicated at every use site.

**Migration per module:**
- LootTable: loot entries reference atoms (:iron_pipe, :chalk etc), roll/2 resolves through catalogue to construct Item.t(). The item_spec type and build_item/1 are the specific targets.
- Combat.Weapon: @registry key becomes a catalogue atom. Public API (stats/1, weapon?/1) currently accepts String.t() from item.name — simpler path is to keep accepting strings and have Weapon do a single catalogue lookup internally. Avoids cascading changes into Inventory.find_item/2 and the server layer.
- Food: same structure as weapon registry, same migration approach.
- Item.new/1 and Item.new/2 accept arbitrary strings and need not change — the catalogue controls only well-known items.

**Migration risks:**
- LootTable tests directly pattern-match raw table entries and must be rewritten to assert on Item.t() values from roll/2. This is desirable but non-trivial.
- Item.t().name is a String.t() used for inventory lookup everywhere in the server and CLI. The catalogue's string names are effectively part of the public API once adopted — changing a display name breaks inventory lookups.
- 'Junk' decision: either all items in production loot tables must be catalogued, or the catalogue explicitly covers only items with non-trivial domain properties (weapons, edibles, consumables). The latter is defensible but must be documented.

**Tests needed:**
- UndercityCore.CatalogueTest: every item name from LootTable (across all block types) is resolvable from the catalogue; every weapon registry item is catalogued; every food table item is catalogued.
- For each catalogued item: constructing via the catalogue and via Item.new(name) produces structs with identical name fields.
- Replace raw loot_table_test.exs structure assertions with roll/2 output assertions (correct Item.t() name and uses count for a known roll value).","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T13:44:36Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-08T13:47:36Z"}

{"id":"undercity-uo1o","title":"Consolidate AP-gating into a single authoritative pattern","description":"Two incompatible AP-gating patterns exist with no documented invariant. move, search, scribble, and attack go through Player.perform/3 which applies the collapse check, lazy regen, and spend in sequence. eat and drop gate AP inline inside Player.Server GenServer callbacks, bypassing perform/3. A developer adding a new action has no guidance on which pattern to follow. Picking the wrong one silently omits either the collapse guard or the regen step. The perform/3 path is documented in docs/actions.md but the inline exceptions are not explained there.","design":"Implement both option 1 and option 2:

1. Document the split as intentional — add guidance to actions.md and inline comments explaining when to use each pattern: Player.perform/3 for block-anchored actions with external side effects; inline handle_call for actions that mutate Player state (inventory, health) where co-application matters.

2. Standardise the return shape — make drop and eat return {:ok, result, ap} to match perform/3, with action-specific data as a sub-tuple (e.g. {:ok, {:dropped, item_name}, ap} and {:ok, {:ate, item, effect, hp}, ap}). Update Gateway, Commands.Drop, and Commands.Eat accordingly.

Option 3 (closures into GenServer) is explicitly out of scope. If the documentation and consistent interface still prove insufficient in practice, a structural enforcement mechanism can be considered at that point.","notes":"## Server Expert Notes

**The two patterns exactly:**

perform/3 path (move, search, attack): Two hops. First sends {:spend_ap, cost} into Player GenServer → Player.exert/3 (checks HP non-zero, applies lazy AP regen, calls ActionPoints.spend/3) → if AP sufficient, persists and replies {:ok, remaining_ap}. Then the action closure runs in the calling process (Actions module), wrapped in {:ok, result, ap}.

Inline handle_call path (drop, eat): Never calls Player.perform/3. Actions dispatch directly to Player.drop_item/2 or Player.eat_item/2, which send distinct messages ({:drop_item, index} or {:eat_item, index}) into the GenServer. Those callbacks call Player.drop/3 or Player.eat/3 domain functions, which internally call exert/3 themselves — AP check and state mutation happen atomically inside a single GenServer call.

Scribble is a third sub-pattern: Player.use_item/3 with a cost argument, sending {:use_item, item_name, cost} as a single atomic GenServer call combining item consumption and AP spend. The block side-effect (writing) happens after the call returns. Closer to perform/3 spirit (AP + effect separated) but the GenServer call bundles AP and item atomicity.

**Key behavioural differences:**
- Atomicity: inline path has AP check and mutation atomic in one GenServer call; perform/3 path spends AP first then runs the closure — if the closure fails after AP is spent, AP is gone but the world-state change may not have happened.
- Return shape: inline path returns action-specific flat tuples ({:ok, item_name, ap} for drop; {:ok, item, effect, ap, hp} for eat). perform/3 always returns {:ok, result, ap}.
- Both paths signal :exhausted and :collapsed the same way. The HP-zero check lives in Player.exert/3 (domain layer) for both paths — no gap there.

**What a unified pattern needs to look like:**

Three options:
1. Cosmetic only: same calling convention at the Actions layer, keep inline callbacks for actions where Player state and AP must be co-mutated. Document intentional split.
2. Closures into GenServer: perform/3 sends the closure into the GenServer itself (exert then execute closure against player state, persist, reply). True atomicity parity. More invasive.
3. Middle path: keep inline callbacks but standardise the return shape to {: ok, result, ap} across all actions, and document that the two wiring patterns are intentional for atomicity reasons.

The bead must resolve which option is in scope. Option 2 is the only path to true atomicity parity but has implications for GenServer interface and testability.

**Risks during migration:**
- If drop and eat are migrated to perform/3 externally, a genuine atomicity gap opens: AP spent in one GenServer call, inventory/health mutated in a second. Existing 'item untouched when exhausted' tests would still pass, but mid-crash behaviour cannot easily be tested with current infrastructure.
- Player.eat_item/2 currently returns {:ok, item, effect, ap, hp} — a 5-element tuple. If eat moves to perform/3, the result payload must carry {item, effect, hp} as a sub-tuple, and both Gateway and Commands.Eat must be updated to destructure accordingly.
- constitution/1 applies ActionPoints.regenerate/2 but does not persist the regenerated AP — a minor inconsistency not introduced by this migration but worth clarifying in the bead.

**Tests needed:**
- Drop returns :exhausted and leaves item in inventory (already exists — any migration must keep it green).
- Eat returns :exhausted and leaves both AP and item unchanged (already exists).
- New test for whichever unified path is introduced: proves AP and inventory/health mutation are consistent across happy path and each failure mode.
- If eat/drop return shape changes to {:ok, result, ap}, new Gateway-level tests needed — existing gateway tests do not cover eat or drop end-to-end.

## CLI Expert Notes

Every CLI command that costs AP routes through Commands.handle_action/3, which catches {:error, :exhausted} and {:error, :collapsed} and renders the appropriate message. The assumption baked into that design is that all AP-costing gateway calls can return those error atoms. In practice, three different AP-gating shapes exist behind the single handle_action/3 abstraction:

1. Gateway.perform/4 (most actions): block guard + AP via Player.perform/3
2. Gateway.drop_item/2 (defdelegate to Player.drop_item/2): no block guard, AP via internal Player call
3. :eat via perform/4: no block guard (special-cased at top of perform/4), AP via Player.eat_item/2

The CLI handles all three the same way and gets away with it because the terminal error atoms are consistent — but the path to those atoms is not, and command authors have no written rule about which gateway path to use for a new AP-costing command. 'Eat' is the confusion case: it is inventory-only yet goes through perform/4 with a special-case bypass, so a new author reading the gateway would not derive a consistent rule.

After consolidation, the CLI itself likely needs no code changes — Commands.handle_action/3 already handles :exhausted, :collapsed, and :not_in_block correctly. The contract the CLI depends on is just that those atoms are returned reliably when conditions are met.

The one edge to verify: if consolidation moves drop_item through the same Player.perform/3 wrapper as everything else, the return shape must stay {:ok, item_name, new_ap} / {:error, :invalid_index}. Commands.Drop pattern-matches on exactly those. Any change to the ok-tuple structure is a breaking change for the CLI.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T13:44:32Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-08T14:10:43Z"}

In Attack.attack/5: AP is spent via Player.perform/3 before Player.take_damage is called on the target — if the target GenServer is unavailable, the attacker loses AP with no combat result. The recovery path (scan_or_restore in Session) does a serial Block.has_person? scan across every world block and is untested for partial-failure scenarios.","notes":"## Server Expert Notes

The architecture has no transactions and cannot be given them cheaply. Every step in both sequences is a separate synchronous GenServer.call to a separate process. The fix must be compensating logic, not a transaction primitive.

Movement has three distinct failure windows, not one: (1) crash between Block.leave and Player.move_to — player absent from both blocks with stale location record; (2) crash between Player.move_to and Block.join — location record updated to destination but presence not yet recorded there (DETS and in-memory Block set inconsistent); (3) crash after Block.join but before Vicinity.build returns — relatively harmless. Windows 1 and 2 are meaningfully different and may need different recovery strategies.

Session already has partial recovery logic (scan_or_restore) that does a serial Block.has_person? scan on reconnect. If a more principled recovery path is introduced, scan_or_restore should be rationalised or removed — otherwise the project has two overlapping recovery strategies with no documented relationship.

Block.join returns block info ({block_id, [player_ids]}) that is immediately discarded in movement.ex line 24. Vicinity.build then makes a second call to Block.info to fetch the same data. If movement is restructured, this redundant call is worth collapsing.

For attack: the race window is between validate_target and Player.take_damage, with AP spent in between. The target's GenServer can stop between those two calls (idle timeout or crash) — currently Player.take_damage on an unregistered process would raise an exit with no explicit guard. The corruption is financial (AP lost on the attacker), not positional, and requires a compensating refund path in resolve_attack.

The fix must preserve the Player.location invariant documented on Player.move_to (@doc states move_to is always called before Block.join). Any reordering of steps must not violate this — location is the source of truth used by Session and tested independently.

Block GenServers do not restart with clean presence state — they reload from DETS, which includes whatever presence list was last persisted. A block crashing mid-leave or mid-join persists the corrupt list. Boot-time reconciliation against Player records does not exist. This is a known boundary — the fix here should document that it does not address block-restart recovery.

Tests will need to simulate mid-sequence process crashes (stopping a GenServer between calls). Existing Test.Helpers setup should be verified to reach individual Block GenServers by name for integration-level tests that assert on post-crash state.

## CLI Expert Notes

The CLI treats every successful gateway response as ground truth and updates state.vicinity or state.ap in place with no subsequent verification. After a move, Commands.Move does %{state | vicinity: new_vicinity, ap: new_ap} — the returned Vicinity becomes authoritative for all future commands. If the server crashes mid-move, the next command passes the stale state.vicinity.id to the gateway, hits :not_in_block, and the player has no way to recover — every action fails with no resync path.

The CLI assumes a gateway response is fully committed. There is no optimistic rollback, no retry, and no reconciliation tick. The 500ms sync_messages subscription polls for inbox messages but does not re-fetch vicinity or constitution — corrupt server state is never self-healing from the client side.

If this fix changes the shape of any gateway return value, the pattern matches in Commands.Move and Commands.Attack will need corresponding clauses. Any new error atom for 'mutation failed and was rolled back' must be added to Commands.handle_action/3 — that is the single normalisation point across all commands. If the error propagates as an unmatched tuple instead, handle_action/3 will pass it to the command-specific callback which will almost certainly raise FunctionClauseError and crash the session.

There is currently no mechanism for the CLI to request a fresh copy of the player's current location from the server. Adding a resync gateway function (returning the player's current vicinity regardless of action) would make the client resilient to any future partial failure, not just the ones fixed by this bead. This may belong in a follow-on bead but the developer should be aware of the gap.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"lreidwilliams@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T13:44:27Z","created_by":"Lewis Reid","updated_at":"2026-03-08T13:54:54Z"}
